title: PostgreSQL Work Claims, SKIP LOCKED, Leases, and Fencing · Go Scenario Checkpoint
summary: A release reviewer compares near-miss Go decisions for postgresql work claims, skip locked, leases, and fencing and accepts only runnable changed-case evidence, explicit non-claims, and named browser, compiler, network, XML, PostgreSQL, race, load, accessibility, deployment, and production transfer gates. Planned artifact: Record independent decisions and explanations for multi-instance fenced fetch-claim protocol. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, ca…
objectives: Claim a bounded due batch with ordered FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED work in a short transaction and release locks before network I/O.
Issue attempt and fencing tokens with owner, expiry, heartbeat budget, and state so stale workers cannot commit.
Finalize success or failure only when source, attempt, token, and current lease match in one affected-row check.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

content.text: A quiz Go team handles case 4257 during an isolated backup restore exercise. Build deterministic Go evidence to prove short SKIP LOCKED claims, fenced leases, conditional finalize, and fault recovery; every goroutine, body, row set, transaction, lease, timer, and pool needs named cleanup ownership; then change one tenant, cursor, reader-state, authorization, cache, or accessibility condition without leaking state. Browser Go uses pure bounded models and original RSS, Atom, HTTP, PostgreSQL, and reader fixtures only. Real DNS, sockets, XML libraries, PostgreSQL, processes, signals, race, load, containers, accessibility review, backups, and production behavior require explicit controlled transfer gates. Competency probe: Claim a bounded due batch with ordered FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED work in a short transaction and release locks before network I/O.
